problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem A ("Hot Bath"): model the spatial-temporal temperature distribution of bath water as it loses heat to the surroundings, and determine the best strategy for a bather to add a trickle of hot water that restores a uniform target temperature without wasting water. This team ("Beneath the Surface") reduced the 3D thermal-fluid problem to a 1D convection-diffusion problem and built three escalating models plus a closed-loop control layer, ultimately prescribing PID control of inlet temperature combined with manually redistributing inlet water upstream of the bather.
models_used: Steady-state uniform-temperature thermodynamic model: First Law energy/mass balance on a control volume (water = system; faucet inlet, overflow drain, bather, and ambient air = surroundings) yielding a single closed-form algebraic expression for equilibrium temperature u (Eq. 10), used mainly to back out physical constants and a qualitative baseline.

Analytical 1D heat (diffusion) equation with TIME-DEPENDENT Neumann boundary conditions and a space- AND time-dependent heat generation term Q(x,t). Solved by (a) 'shifting the data' to homogenize the Neumann BCs, (b) Fourier cosine-series expansion in space, (c) reducing the PDE to a decoupled first-order ODE per Fourier mode v_n'(t)+a(n*pi/L)^2 v_n = Qtilde_n, (d) solving each ODE with the integrating-factor method, then unshifting. Final solution is a Duhamel-type series (Eq. 28).

Numerical 1D convection-diffusion model: finite-difference (forward Euler in time, central difference in space) discretization of du/dt = a*u_xx - epsilon*v*u_x + q/(rho*cp) (Eq. 43), with explicit conditional-stability criteria (Eq. 44). This is the workhorse model because it captures bulk fluid motion (convection), which dominates.

Closed-loop PID controller (Eq. 57) on the average-temperature error u_bar(t)=u_avg-u_obj, driving the inlet forcing f(t)=mdot*(u_in - u(0,t)); gains tuned MANUALLY (Ziegler-Nichols rejected because the numerical scheme has no closed-form transfer function).

Intelligent inlet-water redistribution model: a per-node fraction eta_i^j (sum eta=1 to conserve energy) that lets the bather spread incoming heat over chosen cells (Eq. 52-53); optimal policy = distribute inlet water over the region upstream of the body (x=0 to body start).

Supporting heat-transfer sub-model: classified convection as natural vs forced via Gr_L/Re_L^2 >> 1, then computed surface-specific natural-convection coefficients (top/bottom/walls) from Rayleigh -> Nusselt correlations (Eq. 3), h = Nu*k/L.
key_techniques: Dimensional reduction with preserved 3D physics: collapse to 1D in the flow (x) direction while keeping 3D surface-area heat-loss terms as a lumped source, justified explicitly by cross-sectional averaging.

Peclet-number argument (Pe = vL/alpha ~ 1223 >> 1) to PROVE convection dominates diffusion, which is the central physical insight that justifies discarding the analytical model in favor of the numerical one.
Gr/Re^2 dimensionless-ratio test to justify ignoring forced convection (~558000 >> 1 => natural convection dominates).
'Shifting the data' transformation u=v+U to convert inhomogeneous time-dependent Neumann BCs into homogeneous ones so a Fourier cosine series applies.
Per-mode ODE reduction + integrating-factor solution (variation-of-parameters via time-varying Fourier coefficients) producing a Duhamel convolution integral.
Trapezoidal-rule quadrature to evaluate the Fourier coefficient integrals and the time-convolution integral numerically inside the analytical solution.
Explicit FDM stability analysis (Dx < 2a/(epsilon v), Dt < Dx^2/(2a)) to guarantee bounded error.
Two decoupled objective/metric functions: u_bar(t)=u_avg-u_obj for 'reach target temperature' and sigma(t)=std-dev of the distribution for 'uniformity' — both driven to zero.
Energy-conserving redistribution constraint (sum of node fractions = 1) so the control strategy never invents or destroys heat.

Systematic one-parameter-at-a-time sensitivity analysis (length, width, height, volume factor, diffusivity/soap, inlet temp, mass flow, bather size/position/temperature/h) around a documented nominal-value table.
Cross-model validation: running the same scenario through both analytical and numerical models and explaining where/why they disagree (length sensitivity sign flip).
why_it_won: "From a judge's view, this paper wins on three axes. (1) Modeling depth and honesty: it builds three independent models of escalating fidelity, then uses a quantitative argument (Peclet ~1223) to decide which one to trust — it doesn't just pick the fanciest model, it justifies the choice physically. (2) Rigor that is rare at this level: a genuine from-scratch analytical PDE solution (shifting-the-data + Fourier + integrating factor + Duhamel) sitting alongside a stability-analyzed finite-difference scheme shows graduate-level applied-math command, and the heat-transfer coefficients are derived from real Gr/Re/Ra/Nu correlations rather than guessed. (3) It actually answers the question the prompt asked: it closes the loop with PID control plus an actionable, physically intuitive policy (spread inlet water upstream of the body, use a cup), and proves it drives both objective metrics to zero. The decoupling of 'right temperature' from 'uniformity' into two clean metrics maps directly onto the problem statement, and the consistent sensitivity tables demonstrate the model is robust, not a one-off fit."
section_highlights: "Narrative framing: the entire paper is told as a story about 'Joe Fourier' taking a bath — a memorable hook that also cleverly signals the Fourier method to come, without sacrificing technical seriousness. The summary sheet bullets the three models crisply and states the final recommendation up front. Structure is textbook MCM: Assumptions (clearly enumerated, with model-specific assumptions deferred to each section) -> Geometry -> Thermodynamic constants -> a Nominal-Values table that anchors every later experiment -> three models in increasing fidelity -> a dedicated Model Comparison section -> Control -> Strengths/Weaknesses -> Future Work. Every model section follows the same Theory / Implementation / Verification rhythm, and 'verification' scenarios (both ends insulated -> uniform cooling; faucet on -> convecting heat wave; moving point source) are used to sanity-check expected physics before trusting results. Heavy, well-captioned use of heat maps and dual-axis objective-function plots. Nomenclature table and consistent symbol use. Closes with a polished client-facing 'Superior Products L.L.C.' consulting letter translating the math into plain advice — strong audience awareness."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Fundamentally 1D: all real 3D thermal-fluid effects (buoyancy-driven plumes, mixing, cross-sectional gradients) are assumed away; the team acknowledges this is the main weakness.

No experimental or empirical validation — 'verification' is internal consistency with expected qualitative physics, not comparison to measured bath data; key parameters (body h, soap effect) are admittedly rough.

Crude / physically questionable boundary condition at the drain (u_end = 2*u_(end-1) - u_(end-2), a zero-second-derivative extrapolation) and an inlet BC stitched together for unit consistency rather than derived from first principles.

PID gains were tuned by hand and the authors explicitly state they are NOT optimal ('in no way ... optimal gains') because the FDM model has no transfer function; no Ziegler-Nichols or systematic optimization was possible.

The 'intelligent redistribution' (eta fractions, distributing water with a cup) is physically idealized — it presumes the bather can deposit inlet energy at arbitrary cells without disturbing the flow, which contradicts the no-flow-disturbance assumption.

The analytical model is ultimately discarded for the main analysis, so substantial effort yields a model that disagrees with the trusted one (e.g., opposite sign of length sensitivity) — impressive but arguably over-built relative to payoff.

Several rendered data tables/heat maps in this transcript are garbled or placeholder-like, but in the original these were figures; the modeling claims rest on the equations and narrative, not those tables.
Convection treated as prescribed bulk velocity v from mass conservation, not solved from Navier-Stokes; momentum/turbulence ignored (laminar, inviscid, incompressible assumed).
reusable_patterns: Three-tier model ladder: a closed-form baseline (sanity/constants) -> an analytical PDE solution (rigor showcase) -> a numerical solver (the workhorse that handles real effects), then explicitly justify which one you trust with a dimensionless number. Reusable for almost any continuous/PDE MCM-A problem.
Use a dimensionless ratio (Peclet, Gr/Re^2, Rayleigh) to JUSTIFY simplifications and to decide which physics to keep — converts hand-waving into defensible engineering judgment.

Define separate, decoupled objective metrics that map one-to-one onto the prompt's requirements (here: average-error metric + standard-deviation uniformity metric), then show your strategy drives each to zero.
Anchor all experiments to a single published Nominal-Values table so every sensitivity plot is reproducible and comparisons are apples-to-apples (one-parameter-at-a-time).

'Shifting the data' to homogenize time-dependent Neumann/Dirichlet BCs, then Fourier series + per-mode integrating-factor ODE — a reusable recipe for any forced 1D heat/diffusion PDE with moving boundaries.
Always state and check explicit FDM stability criteria when using forward-Euler explicit schemes; cite the Dt < Dx^2/(2a) type bound.

Verification-before-results discipline: run degenerate scenarios with known qualitative answers (insulated ends -> uniform decay; point source -> localized hotspot) to validate the solver before reporting headline results.
Wrap the whole solution in a memorable narrative persona (Joe Fourier) and close with a client-facing letter — improves readability and judge recall while demonstrating audience awareness.

Energy-conservation constraint as a control safeguard (sum of distribution fractions = 1) so an optimization can't cheat by creating energy — generally applicable to any allocation/redistribution control.
Add a closed-loop control layer (PID) on top of a forward simulation to turn a descriptive model into a prescriptive strategy that answers 'what should the actor DO'.
